title: 25. The Virtues of Being an Object: Touch, Pornography, and Having Bodies: —Conner Habib
text: 25 THE VIRTUES OF BEING AN OBJECT: TOUCH, PORNOGRAPHY, AND HAVING BODIES CONNER HABIB I While you read this essay, your hair will grow and spit will form in your mouth. Your bones and tendons will be shaping themselves and decaying, and masticated food will be dissolving in your stomach acid. Mites will crawl through your eyelashes; your cells will touch each other. You will be and are a wave of motion and movement, of blood and piss and bile. This is science’s description of your body. The problem with it is simple: you thought you were sitting still, reading. When you say hello to or kiss or have sex with someone, are you aware of his or her liver producing bile? Of the shit forming in his or her bowels? People say they want x-ray vision, but they don’t really want to see what’s going on—not under the skin, and not even underneath clothing, where they wouldn’t see perfect bodies, naked and sexual—they’d see nipples squished up against bras, dicks and testicles all mangled up in underwear, and flesh pushed into weird misdirections. So we notice our experience of science’s description of the body: there’s a feeling of distance from it. The descriptions of fluids and processes may seem repulsive or alien, or simply funny or strange. They’re not what we normally encounter as our bodies. This is the deal that science strikes with us. It will tell us, unblinkingly, what is there and what is “real,” but in exchange, we must accept this as the truth, whether we experience it as true or not. We shouldn’t dismiss what science has to tell us, but what if we didn’t have to trade experience for information? A description of the body that approaches our everyday encounter with it, without the scientific description, is something like this: the body is a location for experienced feeling and meaningful sensations. It’s a feeling of hunger or tiredness. It can also be the opposite—being satiated or energetic. It’s a feeling of horniness and strength. When we consider our bodies in our everyday lives, when we’re really connected to them, it’s always through a feeling. Something in our body lights up in our experience. Not distant but scientific concepts such as bile production, but rather as, “I’m hungry.” 1 The phenomenon that can give us the best insight into this is the sense of touch. In his extraordinary book, Our Twelve Senses , Albert Soesman (taking cues from Austrian scientist, philosopher, and mystic Rudolf Steiner) explores touch from a phenomenological perspective. What do we really experience through touch? First, he asks us to imagine what life would be like if we possessed only our sense of touch: With sense of touch I do not feel whether something is warm or cold, whether it is rough or smooth. I do not notice my balance changing. What is it I actually become aware of, then, when I use touch only, leaving everything else out?… I beg your pardon, but would you please try to be a naked worm?… [A] worm in the pitch dark. What would it feel like when for the first time you experienced touch?… You see, don’t you, if you had no senses at all, and for the first time you had this sense of touch, and you “wormed” up against this pebble, you would experience something: “It” … namely an awareness of boundary . 2 In other words, all we experience through touch where we end and where what isn’t us begins. The “it” that Soesman writes about is a sense of boundary and separation. Maybe as you read this, you’ve been touching the chair you’re sitting in or you’re leaning against your lover. Feel. There is you, and there is not-you. Our bodies, through touch, provide us with our individual boundary. This is something we experience without needing to conceptualize it. It’s not like explaining stomach acid, which can be explained and explained, but never sensed in the same way. This is the description of sensation; what is more revealing is the inner truth this sensation gives us. Soesman goes on: There is a peculiar paradox in the sense of touch: on the one hand you can feel that a piece of wood, for example, is something hard, but on the other hand you can feel that it is a fine piece of wood. You can feel: How beautiful it is, doesn’t it feel good when you run your hand over it. This is the peculiar thing: In the hardness we experience being excluded from it, and in the pleasure of touching we experience the desire to make an intimate connection. In this we see an innate desire of the human being; he actually knows that the sense of touch separates him, excludes him from something, to which he is yet related. We are totally closed off from the cosmos … and we end up snuggling up against it, as it were. 3 We touch one another—whether this touch is a caress or a hug or oral sex—it’s how we enact intimacy. And just as we’re all bound up in these bodies, and every time we touch something, we experience our own outline and boundary, so are we all connected. I am separate, as are you. Here’s my boundary, here’s yours, so we have a union with each other—we share in this experience of bodily separation. Each time I touch you, it’s comforting, because this sharing of separation is what makes us (and reveals us to be) not separate. So we see that the outer experience reveals its opposite, which is an inner truth . Science alone can’t quite come to terms with this. In Euclidian geometry, parallel lines never meet. But in the world of meaning, they always meet. They don’t meet in the way perpendicular lines do. Instead, their common point is their form . They meet conceptually because they share the same form. Again, the outer truth reveals an inner truth through its opposite . All this means that material presents itself to us not merely as outside-of-us matter and process, but also as inner experience. In esoteric traditions, the human being is composed of four bodies. The word body may be confusing here, because we’re used to thinking of it as representing a specific material entity. That’s included in the esoteric conception, but added to. Body also means the different aspects of being that evince different universal laws. 4 There’s the body that science tells us we have—the body made of stuff . It’s flesh and bone—basically water- and stone-like substances. This is the mineral body (what I will refer to throughout as the object-body ) of esoteric literature. But of course the body doesn’t change like a river or a stone does—it isn’t merely added to from or changed by forces outside itself. It grows from within . It has a different principle governing life and growth. This growth-body is the etheric body . We also have a body that shows up as the meaning-filled emotional responses. This body of feelings is the astral body and it is also the body responsible for the processes which break down the outside world. Whether it is physical matter or emotional stimuli. The astral body is a bit like a little sun, a fire that breaks everything down (thus astral —for it corresponds to the stars and the cosmos). Finally, we have an I or ego body , which allows us to consider all these processes in thinking. The ego body is the principle in us that allows us to think about thinking, feeling, and doing. Of course, the object-body isn’t ever actually isolated from the other bodies. Even when we sleep—the time the object-body interacts with the etheric body and our cells repair skin and hair—the principles of the other bodies are still in action. It’s a bit like water, which is never truly just H 2 O, but always mixed with other elements in gases and sediment. In trying to conceptualize the object-body separately and in its relation with the other principles at work, blending both the scientific and experiential perspective, we can begin to build a phenomenology of it. To get a full picture of the object-body, we must first consider the scientific viewpoint: the object-body doesn’t act solely though our conscious will. The heart beats, the lungs contract and expand, and our blood moves through us. The body, it is often said, has a mind of its own, and its actions intersect with experience. Anyone who has ever had an erection in public will know immediately what I’m talking about. When it happens, the independent will of the body is glaringly obvious. Then again, it’s not only the penis that reacts to sexual stimulation. We also sweat, out hearts race, we may get a little jump in our stomachs. In fact, the body’s sexual response is often how we know we’re attracted to someone. We may be surprised to find ourselves aroused, but there it is: a draw toward another. This draw can be sustained, and often is. When we see someone we’re attracted to for a second or third time, when we first start dating or after we have sex, the draw stays there. Scientists have widely agreed that there is a combination of factors—including hormones, dopamine, and adrenaline—that work in conjunction with this draw. The attraction becomes very powerful, allowing us to forgive faults we might not normally. Anything that normally annoys us becomes endearing while this draw is sustained. The body’s will makes us extremely kind. But our attitude to this kindness is often flippant. Cognitive scientists and neuroscientists may refer to the above chemical changes as the cause of it all; there’s nothing special about that love stuff, really, just chemicals. Evolutionary psychologists might refer it back to advantageous mating behaviors, leaving out present-day context. In popular culture, we might say, “that’s just infatuation.” We might say that being attracted to someone because of his or her appearance is “shallow.” If someone acts on this initial attraction, we might refer to her or him as a “slut.” A contradiction, then: we love the feeling that the will of the object-body brings, but we don’t hold it in high regard. We think of it as somehow fake. What if we took it seriously? What if, instead of measuring it up to other experiences, we reversed our ethic and held this infatuation stage up as the standard? We would see then that it’s not that these initial feelings are false or fake, it’s that we don’t feel them enough . In other words, we aren’t normally as forgiving and adoring of other people as we are in the initial stages of attraction. What if we were? What if we were as loving and forgiving in our lives as we were while we were sexually aroused? 5 Of course, this isn’t totally possible, because thinking and feeling and growth all interact with the will of the body, and a much more complicated picture of other people eventually emerges. We might stop being sexually aroused by people after a time. Where we once found their accents or the way they chew their food charming, we now find them annoying. This complex picture is also good, because it presents us with an opportunity for intention. What we once did automatically, we have to learn to do consciously. Where once we allowed faults, we must learn to forgive. Where once we couldn’t stop having sex, we have to get ourselves in the mood. We must act with intention. 6 But where did we learn what it means to be at peace with flaws and to love unreservedly? Our first lived-out lesson in that—in loving unconditionally, in excitement in the other person, in forgiveness—comes from the object-body. II The first time I masturbated thinking of a man, I was barely a teenager. I’d masturbated before, but I never really understood why—it was just a feeling contained in myself. I’d push myself into my mattress and consider the strange, warm feeling. Waves up my chest and in my spine, a peaceful feeling afterward. It was unrelated to anything but me. But then my body began to teach me something. I went to the beach with my family and saw my older stepbrother’s friend in the shower. Through the clouded glass of the shower door, I saw his form, the color of his skin, his legs, what must have been his arms, his ass. There were no clear lines, there were shapes and color. I looked at him and saw what was there. I felt inside of me something entirely new, the coalition of light and sound and this … feeling. My body was going crazy, and I had no idea why. My object-body was wiser than the rest of me, it knew things I didn’t, and it was responding to someone else’s body. For gay men, lesbians, and bisexuals (the GLB community), the object-body is a constant teacher of integrity. 7 For many in the GLB community, internal struggle shows up early in life. It’s the struggle between desire and society—between esoteric inner truth and exoteric societal demands. Being attracted to someone of the same sex is at odds with a GLB person’s environment. It could be because they live in a conservative place—but even for those who live in areas that are widely accepting of GLB, the conflicting feeling of difference from peers is often intense. Guilt accompanies this difference, and many try to change or resist their unfolding sexualities—not just in formal ways like camps that purport to turn gay teens straight, but also in private ways like prayer or dating members of the opposite sex. This resolves itself in “coming out,” which isn’t merely a moment of revealing one’s sexuality to others, but sometimes a lengthy process. It involves coming to terms with sexuality inwardly. It usually has a lot of false starts and stops, and the pain in this process—brought on by feelings of being different than peers, of concealing true identity, of becoming acutely aware of something (sexuality) that seems taken for granted by everyone else—can be nearly unbearable. But the object-body won’t be denied. Sooner or later, the object-body’s gestures become stronger than feelings of guilt and societal pressure, and in an amazing act of intention, homosexual and bisexual people affirm their identities privately and publicly, based on inner truth rather than what they are supposed or expected to feel. The body was the first indicator of truth. The body was able to give love and acceptance before the mind. 8 In the isolating and painful mythic period of separation, GLB people feel an outside-ness to their lives. When they work their way back to wholeness, they retain the memory of this separation—and it can be a great teacher of compassion. If the feelings of separation are truly healed, empathy with others in similar (but different) circumstances of isolation or pain becomes available. An inner truth of compassion shows up through its opposite: the sense of separation is what allows GLB people to experience intimacy and empathy. III No cultural phenomenon expresses our confusion about the reality of the object-body better than pornography. Indeed, pornography exposes hypocrisy and power struggles over what the object-body is, how it should be used, and who should decide both. There are parts of the object-body that we regard as having a different quality than others. If this weren’t true, what would the difference be between a sex scene in a mainstream movie and pornography? In a mainstream film, the actors really kiss, sometimes explicitly so, showing their tongues touching. They might be naked, baring breasts, asses, and sometimes even genitals. But as the camera pans down their entwined bodies, one thing is never (or at least very rarely) shown: penetration. In other words, the difference between a movie and porn is about six inches. We live in a world that is saturated in sexual suggestion, but not sex itself. The popular argument goes something like this: pornography isn’t film or art because it is really just exploitation based on “objectification” of people (usually this means women). The argument has changed to hide behind technology. A recent addition to the argument is that porn is destroying relationships. But this part of the argument rose to prominence with the rise of the internet, and these arguments against pornography are really just borrowed critiques of technology: that it creates separation and erodes real human relationships. What’s really underneath arguments against porn, once you pull away all the borrowed supplements, is still objectification. For many critics, these arguments are meant to be self-evident: objectification is bad. Porn is bad. This is easily seen in the many attacks against porn that simply state what is depicted. For example, in the hysteria around Robert Mapplethorpe’s photography, which depicted sexual acts (often featuring naked gay men), attackers would merely describe the act in the photograph. Or in Chris Hedges’s antipornography essay “The Illusion of Love,” 9 he names what he sees and hears as if it presents some sort self-evident truth: “oral sex, vaginal sex, double penetration, and double anal.” He quotes a performer who says during a shoot, “Shove it up my fucking ass,” and “Fuck, motherfucker,” and “Fucking love it.” For some reason, Hedges thinks no explanation as to why this should be problematic is required. Of course, all of this misses an important aspect of our lives: objectification isn’t something that is done to us; we are already and always part object. For those few critics of pornography who don’t believe arguments of objectification are self-evident truths, the rest of the argument goes something like, “It’s a problem because the viewer of porn sees someone only as an object.” These arguments leave out so many questions of context as to leave them impotent. Questions forgotten in this line of reasoning include: Will we react to people in life the way we do to people we watch in porn? Should we? Does all porn have the same affect, even across cultural boundaries (i.e., does straight porn exist in the heterosexual world the same way gay porn does in the gay world?)? Does porn show up in the same way in any and every cultural context? Does it change through time? Because these questions are rarely considered in antiporn arguments, most antiporn arguments are not very useful or complex. But since they are still taken seriously, we must respond to their face value. With the decline of modernistic thinking, multiplicity was revealed as a better model of sanity in psychology. The ability to move from one state to another without radical separation of those states is recognized as a sign of mental health. 10 So psychology works by allowing us to flow between places where we’ve been separated inside. 11 So in some ways it seems true that to see someone as an object without seeing anything else would be troublesome, because it creates a split in thinking. Antiporn thinkers argue that it is tantamount to rape or the Nazi dehumanization of the Jews. But is that the same as recognizing and appreciating the object-body? If not, what is objectification, and who objectifies? As a porn performer, I can say from experience and with confidence that I’ve never been objectified by other performers. Nor have I been objectified by viewers. At least not in a way that seemed to confuse them into thinking I was an object. What happens instead is that I shift in and out of object-hood. Athletes do this too—they engage with their bodies for a specific task. At the end of the game or the shoot, the context changes. When I meet someone who recognizes me for my work with pornography, it usually begins as a recognition of that draw that they’ve felt and then turns quickly into an everyday conversation. No danger of being objectified there. On the flipside, when antiporn critics examine pornography, they often turn their subjects into functions. Again, Chris Hedges’s essay serves well as an example of this often-used tactic. In the essay, the style and fullness of the writing jumps back and forth so that anyone in porn is a mere caricature of a person. Anyone on his side of the argument is fully human. Furthermore, good and detailed research has been done noting that men who watch porn don’t engage in dehumanization. Some of the best of this work (best because it is so detailed) is in Watching Sex: How Men Really Respond to Pornography by David Loftus, which presents in-depth interviews with nearly 150 men who watch porn. 12 Almost none express anything like a split in thinking or the sentiment of objectification. The sample may seem small, but the interviews are detail-rich and as such stand as a glaring contradiction to critics’ reasonings. Unless we want to agree with some of the more vehement porn critics who state that all men are stupid, unaware, or lying about their motivations for watching porn, we have to dismiss this argument based on evidence. As for complaints about studios and studio people exploiting workers, I certainly have observed that. But is this a problem with porn itself? This is a systemic problem of capitalism and socialism and communism. It’s a problem that arises when a society confuses economic values for values about human rights or values about culture. It unfortunately happens in every workplace, and is not specific to porn. Which again raises the questions: Who objectifies? Who destroys and exploits multiplicity? And why? IV People love to say “sex sells.” But this really isn’t honest except in the case of pornography. When you’re driving and you see a billboard of a man in swim trunks drinking beer and a woman in a bikini sitting down on the sand next to him, it’s an ad for the brand of beer in the man’s hand. He might have perfect abs and she might have large breasts. But is this sex? Well, when’s the last time you saw a billboard advertising beer that had a photo of a penis entering a vagina proclaiming BUY BEER next to it? It’s not sex, but the suggestion of it, that is meant to sell. It’s not even just arousal, but a sort of coitus interruptus arousal. Advertising gets you turned on, and how does it consummate the relationship? Instead of showing you sex—which is two people touching, expressing actual intimacy—it shows you a product. The end of the sexual encounter is beer or a computer or whatever other product. So you’re elated and then rerouted. This is dehumanization—but not because there are photos of scantily clad people; that’s not a problem. This is dehumanization because it takes real human emotion—the emotion of the person who sees the ad, an emotion that is aimed at human interaction—and reroutes it into something not human: the computer or the beer. Here and there, this probably wouldn’t cause a problem. But in our culture, arousing and then hiding sex is a calculated, repeated, and basically institutionalized pattern. In a Pavlovian rut, we’re aroused a hundred times, but consummation is never delivered, even in image. The constant bombardment of this sexual rerouting trains us that sex is something separate from life. Indeed, this can be seen in the attitude we have toward our genitals and breasts—that they are parts of our bodies that are seen as separate from us. We even name them sometimes, as if they’re in different worlds entirely. So the easy flow of multiplicity is exploited through a rerouting of sex to product. Add to this the fact that those in charge tell us—not just implicitly through the absence of sexual imagery, but explicitly—that sex is bad. Showing penetration is immoral; it would be indecent, exploitative, and objectifying. This has been going on for so long that we take it for granted. Perhaps one of the best antidotes to this would be the mainstreaming of true sexual imagery. If we took a cue from the Romans, who had sexual images displayed prominently and openly, we’d be much less susceptible to manipulation through arousal. It’s not just capitalist culture that seeks to objectify. This is also science’s mission. It turns us into objects so that it can understand us. Indeed, science may be the most objectifying force in the world. And, of course, it is constantly confusing the object-body for the entire self. Science’s worst crimes are ones that misunderstand a whole organism or system: they’re crimes like genetic manipulation of seeds, dumping poisonous mercury into rivers, testing weapons on humans for experimental purposes. While defenders of science may claim it to be objective, science does not exist in a vacuum. It demands that the world be material, then blends with its objectifying counterpart, consumerism, and commits materialist crimes. After all, what’s to stop anyone from doing anything heinous if all that matters is that we’re just stuff and nothing else, not even experience? On the other end of the spectrum, religion and spirituality often deny the reality of the object-body. The most recognized problems with this are suicide-bombing and the historical and present-day religious wars, in which the body is seen merely as a vessel for spirit. Adherents of fundamentalism don’t have to worry about their bodies, which are a sort of problem for them to cope with before the afterlife. Similarly, many children raised in monotheistic traditions are told that their bodies are filthy and sinful. Not surprisingly, many of these children grow up to be atheists—emphasizing only the materiality they were once instructed to hate. But it’s not only the Abrahamic religions that are guilty of abandoning or mistreating the object-body. In some Buddhist traditions, the body is perceived as a block—a weight of the ego to be overcome. In kundalini practice, the body can become merely a slave to spirit. Like high school boys the night before a football game, practitioners are told not to go all the way. You can orgasm, men are told, but do not ejaculate, or you’ll discharge the vital energy you need to enliven your spirit. While there may be genuine esoteric value in orgasm without ejaculation, it is often turned into a moral prescription. This condemns the object-body to a lower caste than the spirit, rather than viewing it as a dynamic and loving body in and of itself. 13 No real transformation can happen without true engagement. To understand how we relate to our bodies (not just culturally or spiritually, but as individuals), we must be able to simultaneously immerse in and detach from them. By stymying true engagement with the object-body, powerful structures of religion, science, and consumerism create deeper attachment to the body rather than detachment. In cases of religious abandonment of the body, no real transformation is possible because exploration through immersion is denied. Unless we have the freedom to truly engage and understand our object-bodies and its way of being conscious—detailed in this essay through touch and sexual attraction—as well as its way of relating with our other bodies, we’re less likely to interact with it in a healthy way. Instead, radical splits occur and prevent multiplicitous flow. These attempts to destroy multiplicity and banish the object-body are grabs for power by systems of thought—scientific, religious, economic. These systems are often threatened by the body and sex because there is power in cutting off connections to compassion, which the object-body and multiplicity bring us. Fear of gays in the military, for example, was founded on—among other things—the concern that gay men would find the enemy attractive and thus be unable to shoot them. In a sense, the military leaders that feared this were justified. Indeed, it is hard enough to shoot someone you’re not attracted to, and soldiers are subjected to conditioning to help them in this task. 14 It was thought that the added sexual energy of object-body attraction would have been too much. Similar reasoning was in place when leaders resisted women in the military. There are impulses of deep compassion, of relation, that run through our very bodies. In order to turn us against each other, or to bring us under the sway of a power structure, these connections must be erased—otherwise we might tap into the wells of intimacy we share with each other. 15 V So much is in place now to keep us separated that acts as intentional as denying gays military service are no longer needed. Our society is saturated with coitus interruptus advertising, dogmatic fundamentalism, religious atheism, and antiporn crusaders. To pull ourselves away from these inherited and maintained structures, we’ve got to act against them. Luckily, there are tactics that are easy and significant. The root of resistance is remembering that we are all part object, and at the same time, remembering that no part of us is only object. (The map of the four bodies outlined in esoteric traditions is a good organizing principle for thinking this way.) In this way, we can avoid polarity, and instead resonate with the tension between materialism and spirituality. First, get used to the notion that it’s okay to be part object. Many talk about the health of the planet, the health of the body, eating organic food, and so forth. But we forget that our bodies are stuff . They’re not just there for us to desperately try to keep healthy, but instead they are reflections of their own universal laws and principles. And they are part of what makes us ourselves. Once we start to think that way about the object-body we can begin to talk more openly about it. The object-body, in perfect relation to the other three bodies of esoteric traditions, shits and comes and cries tears. After understanding this, we should remember and honor the experience of the body. It’s getting aroused and longs for sex. It’s quivering around an attractive man or woman. It’s a longing for things that we’re told not to pursue. Forgetting the deep reality of either one of these principles—the materiality or the experience—destroys all chance for wholeness. When the issue of gays in the military was in its early stirrings, there was a televised interview with a discharged gay marine who said, “When I’m in the showers, I don’t stare at the other guys, I’m there to take a shower.” He said this to somehow make the military feel safe with his presence. He must have been twenty years old. I was younger, and I thought, “This is a lie.” I knew that because I knew that if I were in the showers, it would be all I could do to stop staring. If that marine had been open about sex at that time, it would have had the potential for radical change. A public statement about real sexuality would have brought us closer together through true understanding. Rather than mimicking the “proper” heterosexual response to other men in the showers, the marine would have stood for inner truth. What kind of world would we have if we came to terms with the fact that we have and partially are this object-self? If we could relate to each other openly and honestly about sex? As an undergraduate at my first college, in western Pennsylvania, I used to visit a cruisy men’s room on campus. Many gay men, students or townies, would go there—there weren’t many other places to go in the little conservative town isolated in the woods. While our straight peers were having sex, making out at parties, holding hands on the campus green, we had to find our own place to meet. At a GLB student meeting, members made teasing comments about the “trolls” who cruised in there. Since I’d been there myself, I’d seen many of the same members of the student group there. It was an obvious hypocrisy, brought on by insecurity. So many of us would go there, but so few of us would talk about it. Sexual and social theorist Amber Hollibaugh once said, “Wherever you have a secret, that is where you are vulnerable.” So I started to talk about it. A freedom rose up in me after that, and I noticed that the demonizing talk about sex started to vanish in my peers as well. Oddly, we treat our bodies, which are out in the open, as a secret. This is a great lie. It’s up to us to be open. If we are publicly vulnerable, if we behave in a way that exposes our vulnerability, we are strong. Again, an outer behavior reveals an inner truth through its opposite. Our duty is to search for and understand the inner truth revealed by the body and by sex. To do this, we have to be open and vocal. We need to make sex and the object-body part of our spiritual, political, and intellectual engagements and activisms. It makes us less susceptible to consumerist religious and materialistic manipulation, it makes us more open to compassion, and it presents us as whole beings. For without bringing our bodies to the conversation—particularly conversations about power and change—how can we hope to be fully honest?
